title: If Your Portfolio Is Down YTD, It’s Because of Your Action Bias, Not the Iran Conflict

And so, when you transition from a risk
on market regime to risk off market
regime or vice versa, as you can see
from how the the factor leadership has
evolved historically, you need to make
major changes in your portfolio to
remain on the right side of market risk.
Now, the market the market regime
nowcasting process is ignorant of how
long we might be in a risk off market
regime. So, that's where you need
forward-looking fundamental research to
suggest, "Hey, hey, I think this is
going to be a cup of coffee. I think
this is going to be a false alarm, or
this is something that can devolve into
something more material." So, definitely
pay attention to this and and to start
taking down risk or taking up risk. How
often does that happen?
On a trailing 1-year, 3-year, 5-year,
10-year, and 20-year basis, about two
times a year.
About two times a year, you actually
need to do something different in your
portfolio, which is
you know, 1/10 the amount of times than
any other investors and certainly other
investment services would prefer you to
do something different in your
portfolio. A lot of folks get their
amygdalas tickled by signal

>> that technically isn't signal. Uh but,
the reality is if you go back and you
study uh market regime change statistics
and all this uh information on a on a
time series basis,
you're talking about really only twice a
year you actually need to do something
differently in your portfolio to remain
on the right side of trending market
risk. And so, that's why our process is
organized like this and the process of
of some of the top buy-side institutions
in the world are organized like this as
well. Okay. And just to underscore it,
we are now in one of looks like we're in
a moment where it's one of those on
average two times of the year where you
actually need to take a change in your
your positioning. 100%. So, you want to
kind of just think about this. If you're
in a bull market,
you know, if we're that means we have a
big enough correction or crash to count
as, you know, taking down risk to to
avoid, you know, kind of chop off the
left side of this. So, let's say you
want to get rid of the last, you know,
2/3 to 85% of the drawdown, you need to
take down risk. And then, you break back
out into the bull market, that's the
second one. And so, in order to, you
know, kind of capture the bulk of the
uptrend, let's say 2/3 to 90% of the
uptrend or at least in Kiss's case it's
97% of the uptrend,
uh you need to do something differently.
And so, it's obviously works in reverse
if you're in a bear market. If you're in
a bear market, like a structural bear
market like the global financial crisis
or the dot-com bust, you know, you're
trending lower, you're trending lower,
but you have a long enough and sustained
enough uptrend to warrant taking up risk
to capture some upside capture uh in the
context of what could be a recovery,
right? You don't know in real time that
it's, you know, the bear market's going
to resume. You just know that, "Hey,
this looks like we're potentially
heading into a new uh bull market." But,
ultimately, uh if you're still in the
bear market, then you know, you're
eventually going to come back down and
that'll be uh kind of second time. So,
you know,
there are other investment services
that'll have you trade every single one
of these wiggles.
That's noise. I would highly recommend
you do not attempt to do that. One, from
a stress perspective, but two, there's
no time series or no there's no data.
I've never I mean, I've read hundreds of
books at this point throughout my
career, uh you know, kind of gravitating
towards what we think are the best
practices from a risk management
standpoint, and I've not come across
anybody saying you can, you know,
hyperactively trade your way into an
on-time and comfortable retirement. I
have seen a lot of Nobel Prize-winning
research that says the more hyperactive
trading you make, the less likely it is
that you're going to retire on time and
comfortable. So,
I'm going to stick with that until I see
something different. Uh I'm I'm always
open to change my mind there on that, uh
but I highly doubt it will. Uh so,
anyway, going back into this process,
you know, just use a few more examples
so we can kind of drive home the the
point we're trying to make, which is
hey, we none of these markets really
knows in isolation what regime we're in,
but if we ask all 42 of them on a daily
basis on a repeatable basis, we can have
a general sense of when the market
decides to change its mind or its mood
and how long that's likely to last from
the perspective of of, you know, what
what from the perspective of correlating
that with our fundamental research
views. So, for example, if we look at
the 10-year uh nominal US Treasury
yield, uh that's bullish. And so, that
bullish VIX condition in the 10-year
nominal US Treasury yield is creating a
generating a point for both reflation
and inflation. It the the bullish VIX
10-year nominal Treasury doesn't know if
we're in a risk on reflation market
regime or a risk off inflation market
regime. It just knows that we're
probably one of those two regimes. And
so, you know, you do that so on and so
forth across each of these 42 markets,
and then ultimately you wind up with,
you know, a high-quality robust process
that essentially tells you, "Hey, the
markets are no longer pricing XYZ on a
trending basis. They are pricing, you
know, ABC or or 123 and on a trending
basis. And whenever you go from risk on
to risk off or risk off to risk on,
that's when you need to make material
pivots in your portfolio to stay on the
right side of factor risk in global
financial markets.
So, the key takeaway is that most of
these markets trend they when they
change trend, they generally don't go
back and forth. And so, it's your job as
an investor to notice when they change
trend faster than other investors so
that you can put your buy orders in for
the things that they're going to
eventually be forced to buy from from a
price momentum standpoint or or risk
management standpoint. Or if we change
trend into a bearish risk on a bearish
risk off market regime, you need to put
your sell orders in before them so that
they eventually sell the things that
need to be sold after you get to get
out. And so, that's what we're trying to
do here is create a robust enough
process to notice changes in trends and
broader market market risk faster than
other investors.
Got it totally. So, if if, you know,
basically the herd movement dictates
where the market's going to go, you've
got all these indicators that you rely
on to say, "Hey, it looks like the herd
is just about to start moving this
direction. Let's get in front of that.
Let's let the herd drive the market
action for us." And then, importantly,
let's let's note when the the herd stops
it's momentum is slowing and looks like
it's going to head in the other
direction and then get in front of that,
too. 100%. 100%. And again, that that
process which you just described, which
is front-running the herd, has no
bearing on our fundamental research
process. These are two separate things.
That's why we have a fundamental
research summary and a quantitative risk
management summary. We separate church
and state just like all any good
buy-side institution would, which is our
fundamental research tells us what we
think is going to happen, and then based
on what we think is going to happen,
generally speaking, what are we going to
like on the long side or what are we
going to dislike on the short side?
The quantitative risk management process
tells us, "Okay, you have this view of
the world and you have the things that
you want and you have the things that
you like and the things that you don't
like, when is it time to actually buy
those things or sell those things?"
Those are two separate choices. For a
lot of investors, that's one choice. I
do a bunch of research and the second I
figure out I like something, I go buy
it. Or I do a bunch of research and the
second I figure it out I like I dislike
something, I go sell it.
That's not what the world's top
investors are doing. I know that for a
fact. I have I've been doing these
meetings for almost two decades now,
thousands of meetings around the world
with many of the top institutional
investors in the world, and I'll tell
you right now, they all separate church
and state, with church being your
fundamental research process, state
being your risk management. And so,
going back to our Kiss and Dr. Mo
signals, what we try to do here at Kiss
and Dr. Mo is say, "Hey,
I'm actually not smarter than the
market. I don't want my investment
performance to be wedded to my
fundamental research summary. I want it
to be wedded to the quantitative risk
management summary because the
quantitative risk management summary
tells me what the herd is doing faster
than most of the other investors in the
herd so that I can keep my buy orders
ahead of theirs and my sell orders ahead
of theirs. I'm not entirely sure, and I
I know this from experience, and we can
talk about why we completely pivoted all
of our uh you know, the investment
signaling that we do purely through the
lens of our, you know, quantitative Kiss
and Dr. Mo signals, is because
I'm not smarter than the market.
Darius Dale is as good as he research
analyst as he is, and you know, I think
I'm one of the world's best uh from a
macro risk management standpoint, I I'm
I'm telling you right now that as one of
the world's best macro risk managers, I
am not smart enough to trade every
wiggle in the market accurately. The
market invariably will beat me to some
theme or some, you know, major risk off
pivot or major risk on uh pivot faster
at least once or twice a year, and I've
noticed that throughout my career, and I
certainly noticed that back in the fall
of 2022, uh which is the last time we um
we were still the uh which is the last
time we were communicating to our
customers to make changes in their
portfolios based on my fundamental
research.
So, the the ever since Kiss came out in
January 20 2023, that was the last uh
you know, prior to that was the last uh
the time we ever had uh investors, you
know, being impacted by my fundamental
research views. Again, we create
fundamental research here at 42 Macro
not because, you know, we we want
investors to do something in their
portfolio. Kiss and Dr. Mo tell them
when and what to do. The fundamental
research tells them, "Hey, this might
happen." So that when you get the signal
to do when and what, you feel confident
enough to dispatch and execute. Mhm.
This Kiss system essentially captures
97% of the upside uh relative to a naked
long buy-and-hold portfolio of 60%
stocks, 30% gold, 10% Bitcoin, uh but it
only has about it only captures about
45% of the downside. And so, that's what
we mean when we say, you know, create a
positively skewed return distribution in
your portfolio. Whenever you have a
positively skewed return distribution in
your portfolio, you're getting rid of
the left side of the distribution, and
if you can get rid of the left side of
the distribution, what you're going to
wind up doing is compounding returns
from a higher net asset value across
multiple market cycles. I don't know if
you guys know this, but the dirty little
secret of compounding is that when you
have a larger sum of money, the same
percentage change creates more wealth.
And so, you ultimately have a faster
growing slope of wealth than you would
if you were suffering from volatility
drag uh throughout that entire process.
And so, that's why Kiss and Dr. Mo are
organized the way they are is because
one, Darius Dale is not smarter than the
market. The market will beat Darius Dale
on many occasions has and has and will
continue to beat Darius Dale on many
occasions, too, what needs to be bought
faster than other investors need to buy
it or what needs to be sold faster than
other investors need to uh sell it. And
ultimately, you know, just adhering and
respecting what the market is trying to
price in on a trend basis creates really
fantastic results. And so, we're really
grateful to be able to, you know, to
offer this to the investing public.
Great. Well, I'm very grateful for it as
well. Again, I'm so glad that we have
this new partnership. So, folks, if you
